Features 6 environment suites and 3 agent tasks across 3 difficulty levels.
Benchmarked 6 popular safe RL methods, showing no single algorithm dominates all tasks.
Quantified critical trade-offs between reward performance and safety constraint violations.
Demonstrated that curriculum learning significantly improves agent performance in high-difficulty safety settings.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: CRAX researchers introduce a JAX-accelerated 3D physics benchmark to speed up safe reinforcement learning evaluation by 100x.

title: AutoPass: Evidence-Guided LLM Agents for Compiler Performance Tuning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) show promise for code compilation tasks, but applying them to runtime performance tuning is difficult due to complex microarchitectural effects and noisy runtime measurements. We present AutoPass, a multi-agent framework for compiler performance tuning that uses compiler and runtime evidence to guide LLM-generated optimization decisions. Rather than treating the compiler as a black box like prior auto-tuning schemes, AutoPass opens up the compiler to the LLM, enabling it to query compiler-internal optimization states and analyze the intermediate representation to orchestrate compiler options. The search process iteratively refines optimization configurations using measured runtime feedback to diagnose regressions and guide latency-improving edits. AutoPass operates in an inference-only, training-free setting and requires no offline training or task-specific fine-tuning, making it readily applicable to new benchmarks and platforms. We implement AutoPass on the LLVM compiler and evaluate it on server-grade x86-64 and embedded ARM64 systems. AutoPass outperforms expert-tuned heuristics and classical autotuning methods, achieving geometric-mean speedups of 1.043x and 1.117x over LLVM -O3 on x86-64 and ARM64, respectively.

processed.summary:
• AutoPass introduces a **multi-agent framework** for compiler tuning that moves beyond black-box approaches by allowing LLMs to query **compiler-internal optimization states**.
• The architecture utilizes an **iterative refinement loop** that uses measured runtime feedback and Intermediate Representation (IR) analysis to diagnose regressions and guide edits.
• The system operates in an **inference-only, training-free** setting, making it platform-agnostic and applicable to new benchmarks without task-specific fine-tuning.
• By bridging the gap between high-level reasoning and **microarchitectural effects**, it provides a scalable way to orchestrate complex LLVM optimization passes.
key_results: Achieved a 1.043x geometric-mean speedup over LLVM -O3 on x86-64 server-grade systems.
Achieved a 1.117x geometric-mean speedup over LLVM -O3 on embedded ARM64 systems.
Outperformed both expert-tuned heuristics and classical mathematical autotuning methods.
Demonstrated successful optimization by analyzing noisy runtime measurements and internal compiler evidence.
Proven effective on both server and embedded hardware without requiring offline training data.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers introduce AutoPass, a multi-agent framework using LLM reasoning and compiler evidence to optimize runtime performance autonomously.

title: CATCH-ME if you RAG: a dataset of Contextually Annotated multi-Turn Counterspeech against Hate and Misinformation Exchanges
summary: Online hate speech and misinformation frequently overlap, yet NLP research has mainly treated them in isolation. While LLMs represent a scalable solution for assisting humans in the generation of counterspeech for both threats, zero-shot models frequently generate repetitive and vague responses, underscoring the need for high-quality examples to steer model generation. However, existing counterspeech datasets against the overlap of hate and misinformation are scarce and limited to single-turn English dialogues, while real-life interactions span across multiple turns and languages. To bridge this gap, we introduce the first large-scale, expert-curated, multilingual dataset of dialogues tackling the intersection of hate and misinformation. To ensure factual grounding, the dialogues are also anchored in verified external knowledge (i.e., fact-checking articles and NGO reports) and include document- and chunk-level span annotations, making it directly applicable for RAG systems. Covering five languages and targeting hate directed at seven marginalized groups, this novel resource enables the training and evaluation of more persuasive, factually grounded counterspeech models.

processed.summary:
*   Introduces **CATCH-ME**, a novel dataset specifically designed for **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** to combat the intersection of hate speech and misinformation.
*   Features expert-curated, **multi-turn dialogues** in five languages, grounded in external fact-checking articles and NGO reports.
*   Utilizes **span-level annotations** at document and chunk levels to improve factual accuracy and model persuasiveness.
*   Addresses limitations in existing datasets by focusing on **multilingualism** and complex, real-world conversational dynamics.
key_results: Creation of the first large-scale, multilingual dataset for combined hate speech and misinformation mitigation.
Supports five different languages and seven marginalized groups for broader applicability.
Implements document-level grounding to reduce repetitive and vague LLM outputs.
Enables fine-tuning of models for more persuasive and factually accurate counterspeech.
Provides a specialized benchmark for evaluating RAG systems in sensitive conversational contexts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CATCH-ME, a multilingual RAG dataset for generating factually grounded, multi-turn counterspeech against online hate.

title: An Infrastructure-less, Control-Independent Solution to Relative Localisation of a Team of Mobile Robots using Ranging Measurements
summary: The ability to localise teams of robots is essential for applications ranging from robotic fleets in unstructured environments to cooperative control and navigation tasks. In such contexts, fixed infrastructure is often unavailable, deployments must be fast and flexible, and system requirements must be minimal. We present a decentralised cooperative localisation algorithm that addresses all these challenges at once. The method is anchor-less, fully decentralised, and, unlike most existing approaches, does not require controlling the robots motion to ensure team observability. It relies only on local odometry, sparse inter-agent ranging measurements, and short-range communication, all of which are widely available in practice. The algorithm adopts a multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework that maintains the entire set of feasible solutions, ensuring robustness under transient unobservable conditions. Moreover, through information sharing, each agent benefits from the estimates of the entire group, even in partially connected conditions.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **decentralized cooperative localization** algorithm for robot teams that operates without fixed infrastructure or external anchors.
- Employs a **multi-hypothesis Bayesian framework** to track multiple feasible solutions simultaneously, ensuring system robustness during periods of transient unobservability.
- Decouples localization performance from robot motion, meaning agents do not require specific **control-dependent movements** to maintain team awareness.
- Leverages **information sharing** across the group, allowing agents to benefit from the collective estimates even within partially connected communication topologies.
key_results: Achieves infrastructure-less localization using only sparse inter-agent ranging measurements.
Eliminates the need for specialized 'anchor' nodes or external positioning systems.
Maintains team observability regardless of the specific paths or motions the robots take.
Provides robustness against sensor noise and data sparsity through a multi-hypothesis approach.
Enables effective cooperative navigation using widely available, low-cost sensors like odometry.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a decentralized localization algorithm enabling robot teams to navigate cooperatively using only sparse ranging and odometry.

title: Automating SKILL.md Generation for Computer-Using Agents via Interaction Trajectory Mining
summary: Explicit skill libraries make computer-using agents easier to inspect, but it remains unclear whether such libraries can be mined from interaction data in a way that improves downstream policies. We study this question through a three-stage pipeline that segments GUI trajectories, clusters segments into candidate skills, and trains a skill-aware policy from the resulting annotations. The mined clusters are readable on the source benchmark: five of eight clusters have at least 0.95 purity against InteraSkill Workflows labels. However, readability does not imply transfer. GRPO improves IW skill-step accuracy only from 18.5\% to 20.5\%, leaves BrowseComp+ essentially unchanged, and underperforms trivial frequency priors on key source-domain metrics. We therefore present the method as a diagnostic study: trajectory mining can expose inspectable skill structure, but the current boundary detector, orderless segment representation, and offline reward model are insufficient for reliable cross-domain policy improvement.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **three-stage pipeline** for computer-using agents that segments **GUI interaction trajectories**, clusters segments into candidate skills, and trains a **skill-aware policy**.
- Investigates the feasibility of generating **explicit skill libraries** (SKILL.md) from raw data to improve agent interpretability and downstream performance.
- Employs **Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)** to refine policies based on mined annotations, identifying critical bottlenecks in **cross-domain transfer**.
- Serves as a **diagnostic study** revealing that while trajectory mining creates readable skill structures, it currently lacks the robustness for significant policy improvement.
key_results: Achieved high cluster purity (above 0.95) for five of eight skill categories on the InteraSkill Workflows benchmark.
GRPO provided only a marginal increase in skill-step accuracy, moving from 18.5% to 20.5%.
Mined skill libraries underperformed compared to trivial frequency-based priors on key source-domain metrics.
Orderless segment representations and current boundary detection methods were identified as primary failure points for policy transfer.
Human readability of mined skills does not correlate with improved downstream policy performance in cross-domain scenarios.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that mining human-readable skill libraries from GUI trajectories currently fails to significantly improve agent policy transfer.

title: Train, Retrieve, or Both? A Four-Arm Head-to-Head for Correct Statutory Citation on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act
summary: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need to be pointed at the provision of law that actually governs a question, with a correct statutory citation. We study this task on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation, asking the operator's question empirically: is fine-tuning enough, or is hybrid retrieval needed? We run a four-arm head-to-head on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid), scored on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. The base model cannot cite the RTA and SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; and the SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Its edge comes from SFT making provision selection more robust to the higher-recall candidate sets that hurt zero-shot RAG. Notably, this cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats a pipeline built on bigger, specialized retrieval models (a larger embedder and a cross-encoder reranker), and a larger/improved training set does not help either: strong statutory-citation performance here does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact zeroes hallucination and clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. All results are on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set and are reported as preliminary.

processed.summary:
• Evaluates a **four-arm head-to-head** architecture comparison (Zero-shot, LoRA SFT, RAG, and Hybrid) for statutory citation using **Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct**.
• Demonstrates that **Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)** increases model robustness by training the LLM to better filter and select provisions from RAG-retrieved candidate sets.
• Validates that **Retrieval-Augmented Generation** is essential for eliminating hallucinations, achieving zero citation errors by construction in legal tasks.
• Proves that **lightweight hybrid pipelines** utilizing bge-small can match or exceed the performance of larger, specialized retrieval models and cross-encoders.
key_results: The SFT+RAG hybrid architecture achieved the highest exact-match score of 0.481.
Retrieval-based methods successfully drove citation hallucinations to zero.
SFT-only models failed due to frequent mis-recall of specific statutory sections.
Lightweight bge-small models matched the performance of larger, specialized legal retrieval models.
Neither increasing the training dataset size nor using specialized rerankers significantly improved the 0.481 baseline.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that hybrid SFT+RAG architectures maximize statutory citation accuracy and eliminate hallucinations in legal AI applications.

title: Quantum ring all-reduce: communication and privacy advantages for distributed learning
summary: Machine learning models have scaled to unprecedented sizes, making training across distributed devices the de facto standard in the field. In this work, we explore how quantum communications can make distributed training both more communication-efficient and information-theoretically private, for both classical and quantum learning models. Ring all-reduce is the foundational communication primitive for large-scale distributed training. We present a quantum version that reduces per-link online communication by a provably optimal factor of two using pre-shared entanglement and superdense coding, without requiring the learning model or gradient computation to change. Beyond bandwidth, the primitive enables privacy guarantees that are information-theoretically impossible for any classical protocol, achieving composable ε-secure aggregation, via verified entanglement, at a 2x overhead in GHZ copies. Our hybrid quantum-classical communication architecture yields simultaneous communication and security advantages for large scale distributed training, regardless of whether the learning itself is quantum or classical. Finally, we characterise quantum advantages in gradient conflict detection for server-to-client communication under bandwidth constraints, a setting that arises after ring all-reduce is completed, when full gradient broadcast to external clients is infeasible. Two variants of the problem admit different separations. For margin-based alignment testing (\textsc{GapIP}_τ), the quantum advantage is quadratic in the margin parameter: \widetilde{O}(τ^{-1}\log P) qubits versus \widetilde{O}(\min(\τ^{-2},P)) bits. For sign-consistency auditing against a private parameter matching (\textsc{TieAudit}_ε), the advantage represents an exponential separation in communication complexity: Ω(\sqrt{P}) bits whereas O(ε^{-2}\log P) qubits suffice.

processed.summary:
* The study introduces a **Quantum Ring All-Reduce** architecture that utilizes **superdense coding** and pre-shared entanglement to optimize distributed machine learning communication.
* It achieves a provably optimal 2x reduction in **online communication bandwidth** per link without requiring modifications to the underlying classical or quantum gradient computations.
* The framework enables **information-theoretic privacy** through verified GHZ states, providing epsilon-secure aggregation guarantees that are unattainable by classical protocols.
* The researchers identify significant quantum advantages in **gradient conflict detection**, offering exponential communication savings for sign-consistency auditing between servers and clients.
key_results: Provable 2x reduction in per-link online communication for distributed training via quantum entanglement.
First implementation of information-theoretically private aggregation (epsilon-secure) with only a 2x GHZ copy overhead.
Quadratic quantum advantage for margin-based alignment testing (GapIP_tau) in gradient broadcasting.
Exponential separation in communication complexity for sign-consistency auditing (TieAudit_epsilon) versus classical bits.
Full compatibility with existing classical learning models and distributed training primitives like ring all-reduce.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce quantum ring all-reduce to halve training bandwidth and enable information-theoretically private distributed learning.

title: SoftSkill: Behavioral Compression for Contextual Adaptation
summary: Agent skills are commonly deployed as natural-language Markdown files that encode answer policies, evidence-use habits, and task procedures. These files are readable and portable, but they are consumed indirectly: for each task instance, a frozen language model must translate a long textual artifact into generation-time behavior. This paper asks whether a natural-language skill can instead initialize a compact continuous context object, refined by a trainable soft delta while the base model remains frozen. We propose SoftSkill, a frozen-backbone method that tunes such soft skills with next-token prediction and deploys them as latent behavioral priors at inference time. In our main single-round setting, a length-32 SoftSkill prefix on Qwen3.5-4B improves over no-skill prompting by 8.3 points on SearchQA, 42.1 points on LiveMath, and 1.3 points on DocVQA. Relative to SkillOpt, SoftSkill improves accuracy by 5.2 points on SearchQA and 12.5 points on LiveMath, while replacing hundreds to thousands of Markdown skill tokens with a few virtual tokens. We further study agentic execution as a harder boundary case, where sparse trajectory imitation provides useful signal but does not yet robustly compress long-horizon procedural behavior. More broadly, the results suggest that some task skills are better treated not as additional Markdown to be reinterpreted at inference time, but as compact latent controls over how a frozen model enters the task.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **SoftSkill**, a method to compress natural-language agent instructions into compact **continuous context objects** that act as latent behavioral priors.
- Utilizes a **frozen-backbone** approach where a 32-token soft prefix is tuned via next-token prediction, replacing the need for long Markdown skill files.
- Replaces hundreds or thousands of textual tokens with a small set of **virtual tokens**, significantly reducing inference overhead while maintaining or improving task performance.
- Evaluates the method on single-round tasks like **SearchQA** and **LiveMath**, while identifying current limitations in robustly compressing **long-horizon agentic trajectories**.
key_results: Improved SearchQA performance by 8.3 points over no-skill prompting using Qwen3.5-4B.
Achieved a 42.1 point gain on LiveMath compared to standard zero-shot prompting.
Outperformed the SkillOpt baseline by 12.5 points on LiveMath with significantly fewer tokens.
Maintained behavioral control in multimodal settings with a 1.3 point boost on DocVQA.
Successfully compressed thousands of Markdown instructions into just 32 virtual soft-prompt tokens.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce SoftSkill, replacing verbose Markdown agent instructions with compact soft prefixes to improve frozen model behavioral control.

title: A Model-Driven Approach for Developing Families of Reinforcement Learning Environments
summary: Virtual training environments are software-intensive systems in which reinforcement learning (RL) agents learn, adapt, and demonstrate meaningful behavior. Virtual training environments offer a safe and cost-efficient alternative to training agents in real-world settings. However, to converge, most realistic RL problems require training in multiple, mostly similar but slightly different environments - i.e., families of environment variants. The typical development process of environment families is a labor-intensive and error-prone manual endeavor that does not scale well. To alleviate these issues, in this paper, we propose a model-driven approach for developing families of RL training environments. To obtain the family of environments, we develop an approach and prototype tool. In our approach, a hybrid genetic algorithm - a combination of population-based global search and heuristic local search - generates environment families. Mutations and constraints are expressed as model transformations and are operationalized into a search process by a state-of-the-art model transformation engine. We demonstrate the soundness of our approach in a wildfire mitigation scenario and curriculum learning - a particular learning paradigm that relies on environment families.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **model-driven engineering (MDE)** framework to automate the generation of RL environment families, significantly reducing manual coding effort.
- Employs a **hybrid genetic algorithm** that integrates global population-based search with heuristic local search to discover diverse and valid environment configurations.
- Utilizes a **model transformation engine** to operationalize constraints and mutations, ensuring all generated training scenarios remain structurally sound.
- Demonstrates effectiveness in **curriculum learning** contexts, specifically within a complex wildfire mitigation simulation.
key_results: Development of a prototype tool for automated RL environment synthesis.
Successful integration of genetic algorithms with model-driven transformation rules.
Reduced human error in the design of complex, multi-variant simulation environments.
Validation of the framework through a curriculum learning paradigm in wildfire scenarios.
Establishment of a scalable pipeline for generating heterogeneous training data for RL agents.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Eindhoven University of Technology researchers introduce a model-driven framework using genetic algorithms to automate diverse RL training environment generation.

title: AgenticDB: Agentic Performance Reconfiguration for Database Workloads
summary:
Database configuration tuning is critical for workload performance, but practical tuning on real deployments remains difficult. Existing automatic tuners mostly formulate tuning as iterative search over DBMS knob values. This formulation leads to high execution cost, prematurely narrows the configuration space, and leaves practical requirements insufficiently addressed: diagnosing runtime bottlenecks from system feedback, exploring OS-level reconfiguration opportunities, executing changes robustly, and learning from previous trials and tasks.
  We propose AgenticDB, an agentic framework for database workload reconfiguration. AgenticDB implements a context-grounded harness that interacts with the target database environment by proposing DBMS- and OS-level changes, applying them under safety constraints, observing workload performance and runtime states, and using execution feedback to guide subsequent decisions. This runtime interaction enables AgenticDB to diagnose bottlenecks, explore a broader DBMS- and OS-level reconfiguration space, avoid unsafe or unsupported actions, and accumulate experience within and across reconfiguration tasks. As a result, AgenticDB turns database tuning into a self-refining reconfiguration process in which runtime feedback iteratively improves later decisions.
  We conduct extensive experiments on MySQL and PostgreSQL using YCSB, Sysbench, and TPC-H workloads. The results show that AgenticDB achieves the best final performance on all evaluated workloads, improving over the strongest baseline by 118.1% on average and reducing aggregate time-to-best by 22.6%. The results also demonstrate that its OS-level action space, robust execution lifecycle, and memory-enhanced planning contribute to more effective and practical database reconfiguration.

processed.summary:
• **AgenticDB** introduces a context-grounded agentic framework that moves beyond traditional knob-tuning to autonomous, feedback-driven database reconfiguration.
• The architecture employs a **runtime interaction harness** that diagnoses system bottlenecks and applies both **DBMS- and OS-level changes**.
• It utilizes **memory-enhanced planning** and safety constraints to iteratively refine decisions based on execution feedback and historical task data.
• The framework demonstrates superior performance on MySQL and PostgreSQL by integrating a **robust execution lifecycle** into the tuning process.
key_results: 118.1% average performance improvement over the strongest existing baselines.
22.6% reduction in aggregate time-to-best metrics across benchmarks.
Successful expansion of the tuning space to include critical OS-level configurations.
Demonstrated effectiveness on industry-standard workloads including YCSB, Sysbench, and TPC-H.
Validation of memory-enhanced planning for accumulating experience across different reconfiguration tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce AgenticDB, an agentic framework that optimizes database performance by 118% through autonomous DBMS and OS reconfiguration.

title: Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration for Frozen Pose-Flow Video Anomaly Detection
summary: Pose-flow video anomaly detectors are attractive for one-class surveillance because they provide likelihood-based rankings for tracked skeleton windows. However, a single likelihood score may hide multimodal normal behavior and be sensitive to pose-observation noise. We study a frozen-detector setting in which the pose-flow backbone, cached skeleton tracks, and evaluation pipeline are fixed. Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration (RPC) is a post-hoc score calibration method for this setting. It adds a standardized nearest-prototype deviation in the frozen latent space to the standardized flow score, and uses keypoint confidence only to gate this added geometric evidence. Thus, RPC preserves the original density signal while correcting the ranking with empirical normal-mode structure under pose reliability. Across two frozen pose-flow backbones and four datasets, RPC improves frame-level AUROC in all eight backbone-dataset pairs, with gains ranging from 0.34 to 4.49 percentage points and averaging 2.03 points. Ablation and reliability analyses show that prototype deviation is the main corrective signal, while reliability gating is most useful when pose observations are less trustworthy. These results suggest that lightweight post-hoc calibration can strengthen cached pose-flow systems when retraining or reproducing the full pose pipeline is impractical.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **Reliability-Aware Prototype Calibration (RPC)**, a post-hoc score calibration technique for frozen **pose-flow video anomaly detection** backbones.
• Integrates **standardized nearest-prototype deviation** in the latent space with original flow scores to capture multimodal normal behaviors.
• Introduces **reliability gating** based on keypoint confidence to weight geometric evidence, mitigating sensitivity to pose-observation noise.
• Demonstrates performance gains across multiple benchmarks without requiring retraining of the underlying pose-estimation or flow pipelines.
key_results: Average improvement of 2.03 percentage points in frame-level AUROC across eight tested pairs.
Maximum AUROC gain of 4.49 percentage points achieved on specific benchmarks.
Successful calibration of frozen systems where retraining is impractical or too costly.
Ablation confirms prototype deviation as the primary corrective signal for detection accuracy.
Reliability gating effectively filters noise in low-quality pose observation scenarios.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce RPC, a post-hoc calibration method that improves pose-flow anomaly detection accuracy using reliability-gated prototype deviations.

title: Statistical Properties of Training & Generalization
summary: Deep learning has managed to evade numerous intuitions from classical statistics to achieve unprecedented performance on a number of real-world tasks. In this article, we investigate the key features and surprises of deep learning from a physics-informed perspective, taking care to point out and justify where possible the many choices inherent in constructing a deep learning model. In particular, we review the phenomenon of neural scaling laws and discuss their interplay with the constraints and inductive biases which may be present when applying machine learning to problems in physics.

processed.summary:
- Analyzes the divergence between **deep learning performance** and **classical statistical intuition**, specifically regarding how overparameterized models avoid overfitting.
- Reviews the mechanics of **neural scaling laws** and their role in predicting performance as a function of compute, data, and model parameters.
- Examines the integration of **inductive biases** and physical constraints to improve the robustness and sample efficiency of machine learning models.
- Provides a **physics-informed perspective** on the training dynamics and generalization capabilities of modern neural architectures.
key_results: Deep learning models bypass classical statistical limitations through implicit regularization and architectural priors.
Neural scaling laws demonstrate predictable power-law improvements in loss across multiple orders of magnitude.
Inductive biases are critical for applying machine learning to physical domains where data is sparse or constrained.
Generalization behavior is heavily influenced by the interplay between model capacity and the underlying data manifold structure.
The research justifies architectural choices by mapping model training to physical system optimization processes.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate how physics-informed frameworks and neural scaling laws explain the counter-intuitive generalization success of deep learning.

title: PsyScore: A Psychometrically-Aware Framework for Trait-Adaptive Essay Scoring and ZPD-Scaffolded Feedback
summary: Effective Automated Essay Scoring (AES) are expected to support both reliable assessment and actionable instructional feedback. However, existing approaches often treat scoring and feedback as separate components: neural scoring models provide limited interpretability, while Large Language Model (LLM)-based feedback is typically insensitive to learners proficiency levels. To address this fragmentation, this work proposes PsyScore, a psychometrically-aware framework that integrates diagnostic assessment with instructional scaffolding through a shared latent ability representation. PsyScore comprises three key modules: a Trait-Adaptive Neural IRT Scorer that incorporates the Graded Partial Credit Model (GPCM) into a neural architecture, enabling the precise estimation of student ability while maintaining psychometric interpretability, a ZPD-Scaffolded Feedback Generator, which conditions multi-agent feedback strategies on the diagnosed ability parameter to adapt instructional focus across different proficiency levels, and a Multi-Perspective Feedback Evaluation Strategy that assesses feedback quality via pairwise preference judgements and student revision simulations. Experiments on the ASAP++ dataset demonstrate that PsyScore achieves competitive scoring performance while providing more pedagogically aligned feedback.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **PsyScore**, a psychometrically-aware framework that bridges diagnostic assessment and instructional feedback via a shared **latent ability representation**.
- Features a **Trait-Adaptive Neural IRT Scorer** integrating the **Graded Partial Credit Model (GPCM)** into a neural architecture to provide precise and interpretable scoring.
- Utilizes a **Multi-agent Feedback Generator** that scaffolds instructional content according to the **Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD)**, ensuring feedback is proficiency-sensitive.
- Employs a novel **Multi-Perspective Evaluation** strategy using pairwise preference judgments and LLM-driven student revision simulations to validate pedagogical effectiveness.
key_results: Achieves competitive scoring performance against state-of-the-art Automated Essay Scoring (AES) models.
Successfully integrates psychometric IRT parameters into neural architectures for enhanced interpretability.
Demonstrates superior alignment of instructional feedback with specific learner proficiency levels.
Validates feedback quality through a combination of pairwise preferences and revision simulation metrics.
Proves the effectiveness of multi-agent orchestration in delivering diversified instructional scaffolding.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce PsyScore, a framework combining Neural IRT and multi-agent systems to provide psychometrically-aligned, proficiency-sensitive essay feedback.

title: ELVA: Exploring Ranking-Driven Universal Multimodal Retrieval
summary:
Leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) via contrastive learning has become a mainstream paradigm for improving the performance of Universal Multimodal Retrieval (UMR). However, previous works have ignored the grain blindness when adapting the contrastive paradigm into retrieval tasks. Grain blindness refers to the tendency of the model to overlook grain-level information contained in the query, which is crucial for effectively handling complex queries. This stems from contrastive learning treating samples as a binary classification (positive/negative), while ignoring the different information carried by each negative sample. To address this, we argue that negatives should be treated differently according to their similarity to the positive sample, enabling the model to learn distinct grain information from each negative. In this paper, we introduce a simple but effective framework, called ELVA, a novel rule-based RL framework that mitigates grain blindness through ranking-driven MLLMs. 1) Instead of relying on reward models, we extend Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards
  (RLVR) to retrieval tasks, allowing the model to explore new ranking behaviors without explicit ranking labels. 2) By utilizing rule-based rewards, our approach jointly optimizes the ranking of negative samples while enlarging the similarity gap between positive and negative. To more precisely measure grain blindness, we further introduce MRBench, a new benchmark specifically designed for multi-grain query scenarios. ELVA achieves state-of-the-art results across standard retrieval benchmarks, and its notable 13.1% improvement on MRBench further demonstrates its effectiveness in alleviating grain blindness.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **ELVA**, a rule-based Reinforcement Learning framework designed to solve **grain blindness** in Universal Multimodal Retrieval (UMR).
- Extends **Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR)** to retrieval tasks, allowing MLLMs to optimize ranking behaviors without requiring explicit ranking labels.
- Utilizes **rule-based rewards** to treat negative samples as a spectrum based on similarity, widening the gap between positive and negative results across varying granularities.
- Implements a new evaluation methodology via **MRBench**, specifically targeting multi-grain query scenarios to test model precision.
key_results: Achieved State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance across multiple standard multimodal retrieval benchmarks.
Delivered a 13.1% improvement on MRBench compared to previous contrastive learning baselines.
Successfully mitigated grain blindness by optimizing the ranking of negative samples rather than treating them as binary classifications.
Validated the effectiveness of verifiable rewards in retrieval, eliminating the need for separate reward models.
Demonstrated enhanced query-to-result alignment through ranking-driven Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs).
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ELVA, a ranking-driven RL framework that eliminates grain blindness to improve multimodal retrieval accuracy.

title: Lagrange: An Open-Vocabulary, Energy-Based Sparse Framework for Generalized End-to-End Driving
summary: Scaling end-to-end autonomous driving to complex, open-world environments requires perceptual models that generalize to anomalous scenarios and planners that produce kinematically valid trajectories. Existing paradigms face a distinct dichotomy between representational efficiency and generalization capacity. Dense models (e.g., occupancy networks), while geometrically robust, incur critical computational bottlenecks and struggle with high-level semantic reasoning. Conversely, sparse, query-based planners are efficient but reliant on closed-set definitions, rendering them vulnerable to out-of-distribution (OOD) events. Although recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models offer open-vocabulary reasoning, their autoregressive, discrete token generation fundamentally conflicts with the continuous, high-frequency control requirements of vehicle dynamics. To address this, we propose Lagrange, an open-vocabulary, computationally sparse driving framework based on Masked Latent Fields (MLF). Rather than relying on dense volumetric reconstructions or closed-set query mechanisms, Lagrange exploits Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to encode class-agnostic object proposals into continuous semantic visual tokens. We introduce an intent-driven masked cross-attention module that temporally filters irrelevant entities, decoding the attended tokens into an implicit continuous energy field defined over spatial coordinates. By framing decision-making as a Lagrangian action minimization problem spanning this energy field, we enforce strict compliance with vehicle kinematics while executing collision avoidance. Extensive offline evaluations on both standard (nuScenes) and long-tail (CODA) benchmarks demonstrate that Lagrange establishes a promising framework for robust, interpretable, and kinematically feasible open-world autonomy.

- Proposes **Lagrange**, a sparse framework using **Masked Latent Fields (MLF)** to bridge the gap between efficient sparse planners and open-vocabulary generalization.
- Leverages **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** to encode class-agnostic proposals into continuous semantic tokens, enabling detection of out-of-distribution (OOD) objects.
- Implements an **intent-driven masked cross-attention** module for temporal filtering and decodes tokens into an **implicit continuous energy field** for spatial reasoning.
- Formulates trajectory planning as a **Lagrangian action minimization** problem to ensure kinematic feasibility and collision avoidance in complex environments.

Demonstrates superior robustness in long-tail scenarios via the CODA dataset evaluation.

Ensures kinematically valid trajectories through a physics-informed action minimization framework.

title: Editorial Alignment: A Participatory Approach to Engaging Editorial Expertise in LLM-mediated Knowledge Dissemination
summary: The emergence of LLM-driven information services is reshaping the conditions under which public knowledge institutions operate, threatening to absorb the editorial function these institutions exist to exercise. While LLMs offer powerful new affordances for knowledge dissemination, editorial authority is challenged by pretrained LLMs that arrive already aligned with the values and dissemination strategies of their commercial developers. This paper investigates editor participation in re-aligning LLM interfaces to editorial standards through design workshops, in a case study where we design and implement an LLM-enabled encyclopedia interface with a Nordic public knowledge institution. We introduce editorial alignment as a design practice within Participatory AI, framing AI alignment as a design process and positioning the editorial standard as a design artefact that translates editorial practice and values into alignment objectives for technical implementation. Last, we discuss how editorial alignment can create space for ongoing participation and give editors agency in LLM-mediated knowledge dissemination.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **Editorial Alignment** as a Participatory AI framework to bridge the gap between commercial LLM default values and institutional editorial standards.
* Frames the **editorial standard** as a technical design artifact that translates qualitative human values into alignment objectives for LLM-mediated interfaces.
* Implements a case study with a **Nordic public knowledge institution** to re-align LLM-driven encyclopedia interfaces with professional editorial authority.
* Focuses on maintaining **Institutional Agency** by treating AI alignment as a collaborative design process rather than a purely black-box technical task.
key_results: Defined 'Editorial Alignment' as a specialized branch of Participatory AI.
Established the 'Editorial Standard' as a bridge between human expertise and LLM constraints.
Developed a methodology for using design workshops to influence AI alignment objectives.
Demonstrated a prototype LLM-enabled encyclopedia interface built with domain-expert input.
Identified key friction points between pre-trained commercial AI values and public service dissemination goals.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce 'Editorial Alignment' to help public institutions maintain authoritative control over LLM-driven knowledge dissemination processes.

title: SPOT-E: Test-Time Entropy Shaping with Visual Spotlights for Frozen VLMs
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) often underperform on evidence intensive tasks because decisive visual evidence are small, localized, and easy to overlook, leading to failures in evidence readout even when high-level reasoning is intact. Prior inference-time visual interventions can improve grounding without retraining, but they are largely open-loop and lack a mechanism to verify whether highlighted evidence is actually used. We study answer-span prediction entropy as a model-internal feedback signal and show that naive entropy minimization is ambiguous, since low entropy may arise from evidence-grounded confidence or shortcut collapse. To resolve this ambiguity, we introduce low-entropy anchors and an entropy-shaping objective that reduces answer uncertainty while preserving baseline high-confidence tokens. We instantiate this principle in SPOT-E, a plug-and-play test-time method that produces question-conditioned spotlights, optimized per instance via light-weight tuning based on Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Across all benchmarks and different VLM families, SPOT-E yields consistent gains and improved robustness under visual corruptions. Code is publicly available at: \url{https://github.com/YinBo0927/SPOT-E}

processed.summary:
• Introduces **SPOT-E**, a plug-and-play test-time method that generates **question-conditioned visual spotlights** to help frozen VLMs focus on small, localized evidence.
• Implements an **entropy-shaping objective** that uses model-internal feedback to reduce answer uncertainty while employing **low-entropy anchors** to prevent shortcut collapse.
• Employs **Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)** for efficient, instance-level optimization of spotlight parameters, marking a novel application of RLHF techniques at inference time.
• Enhances **visual grounding** and robustness without requiring retraining or fine-tuning of the base vision-language model parameters.
key_results: Consistent performance improvements across multiple VLM families and diverse benchmarks.
Significant gains in evidence-intensive tasks where localized visual data is critical.
Enhanced model robustness against various types of visual corruptions.
Successful validation of answer-span prediction entropy as a reliable model-internal feedback signal.
Optimization via GRPO proves effective for light-weight, instance-specific visual intervention.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Yin et al. introduce SPOT-E, utilizing GRPO-driven entropy shaping to optimize visual spotlights for evidence-heavy multimodal reasoning.

title: A Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization
summary: Many decision-making problems in computing and networking systems can be naturally formulated as cost-minimization problems under performance constraints. In dynamic environments, reinforcement learning (RL) is often used to solve such problems at runtime by embedding both costs and constraint violations into a single scalar reward through weighted penalty terms, following a Lagrangian-inspired formulation. However, in this context the behavior of the learned policy critically depends on the choice of these weights, which are typically selected manually. This makes it difficult to identify an appropriate trade-off between optimizing the primary objective and effectively avoiding constraint violations, particularly in non-stationary environments where their relative importance may change. This paper presents MAMO (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization), an approach to tackle this balancing problem through multi-agent RL. MAMO decouples task execution from objective design by formulating the selection of reward weights as a learning problem, providing a !rst step towards more autonomous and robust RL-based solutions for constrained optimization problems in dynamic environments.

processed.summary:
* **MAMO** (Multi-Agent system for Multi-Objective constrained optimization) utilizes a dual-agent architecture to automate the selection of reward weights in reinforcement learning.
* The framework **decouples task execution** from objective design by assigning the weight-selection process to a dedicated learning agent.
* It addresses the limitations of traditional **Lagrangian-inspired formulations** which typically rely on static, manually tuned penalty parameters.
* The system is designed to maintain high performance in **non-stationary environments** where the relative importance of constraints and primary objectives fluctuates.
key_results: Eliminates the requirement for manual weight tuning in multi-objective RL tasks.
Proposed a manager agent that learns to optimize the trade-off between costs and performance constraints.
Developed a robust solution for constrained optimization in dynamic computing and networking systems.
Achieved better adaptability in non-stationary environments compared to static penalty methods.
Formalized reward weight selection as a distinct learning problem within a multi-agent framework.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce MAMO, a multi-agent system that dynamically learns optimal reward weights for robust, constrained RL optimization.

title: ScholarQuest: A Taxonomy-Guided Benchmark for Agentic Academic Paper Search in Open Literature Environments
summary: Academic paper search is a core step in scientific research, and LLM-based search agents are emerging as a promising paradigm for iterative, intent-driven literature exploration. However, existing benchmarks are insufficient for systematically evaluating agentic academic search under realistic open literature environments. We propose ScholarQuest, a large-scale, taxonomy-guided benchmark for agentic academic paper search. ScholarQuest is constructed from over 1,000 computer science topics and four representative research intents, including method-oriented, setting-anchored, comparison-based, and scope-controlled queries. It further provides scalable answer construction and a shared retrieval backend ScholarBase for reproducible evaluation. Benchmarking results show that agentic methods outperform single-shot retrieval baselines, yet the best-performing agent only achieves 0.314 Recall@100 and 0.355 Recall@All, indicating substantial room for improvement. In addition, analyses of search efficiency, intent-level robustness, and failure cases further highlight the benchmark's ability to provide multi-dimensional evaluation signals for academic paper search agents.

processed.summary:
- Introduction of **ScholarQuest**, a large-scale, taxonomy-guided benchmark designed to evaluate **agentic academic paper search** across complex, open-literature environments.
- Architecture includes **ScholarBase**, a reproducible retrieval backend that allows for standardized evaluation of multi-step, intent-driven search behaviors.
- The framework categorizes queries into four **research intents**: method-oriented, setting-anchored, comparison-based, and scope-controlled to simulate realistic scientific exploration.
key_results: Agentic methods consistently outperform traditional single-shot retrieval baselines.
The top-performing agent achieved only 0.314 Recall@100, indicating a massive performance gap.
Recall@All peaked at 0.355, highlighting the difficulty of exhaustive literature search for current LLMs.
Evaluation identified specific failure cases in robustness across different intent-level queries.
Results demonstrate a clear trade-off between search efficiency and the depth of retrieved literature.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ScholarQuest researchers introduce a taxonomy-guided benchmark revealing that current AI agents struggle to navigate complex academic literature environments effectively.

title: QMFOL: Benchmarking Large Language Model Reasoning via Quantifiable Monadic First-Order Logic Test Case Generation
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have made significant progress in reasoning, particularly in deductive reasoning, which is crucial for high-stakes decision-making. As models improve, evaluation benchmarks should evolve to keep pace. However, existing benchmarks lack fine-grained control over logical complexity and struggle to balance semantic diversity with logical consistency.
  To address these issues, we propose QMFOL, an automated framework for generating monadic first-order logic reasoning tasks with quantifiable and controllable complexity. It constructs formal logical structures using conjunction and disjunction patterns, enabling precise control over reasoning depth, width, label types, and distractors. These structures are then translated into natural language via LLMs, with logical consistency ensured through round-trip verification using an external prover. Based on our framework, we build QMFOLBench, a benchmark comprising 2880 instances with 960 configurations across diverse logical and semantic dimensions. Evaluations on six large reasoning models (LRMs) and two LLMs show that performance degrades and computational overhead increases with rising logical complexity. Models perform better on True-labeled tasks than on False or Unknown ones, and exhibit sensitivity to semantic variation. Overall, QMFOL offers a scalable and reliable approach for constructing deductive reasoning benchmarks with controllable complexity, enabling more precise evaluation of reasoning capabilities in modern language models.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **QMFOL**, an automated framework for generating **Monadic First-Order Logic** reasoning tasks with precise control over logical complexity variables.
- Utilizes a **round-trip verification** pipeline that translates formal logic into natural language via LLMs and validates consistency using an **external prover**.
- Provides a scalable method to construct the **QMFOLBench** dataset, featuring 2880 instances that vary by reasoning depth, width, and distractor presence.
- Enables a more granular assessment of **Large Reasoning Models (LRMs)** by quantifying the relationship between logical structure and model performance.
key_results: Model performance significantly degrades as logical depth and width increase.
Computational overhead and token usage scale proportionally with task complexity.
Models achieve higher accuracy on 'True' labels than on 'False' or 'Unknown' outcomes.
Reasoning capabilities are sensitive to semantic variations despite identical underlying logic.
Evaluation of six LRMs reveals critical gaps in handling complex deductive reasoning patterns.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce QMFOL to generate quantifiable logic benchmarks that reveal performance decay in reasoning models as complexity scales.

title: Learner-based Concept Drift Detection: Analysis and Evaluation
summary: Machine learning algorithms deployed for evolving streaming environments must handle the non-stationary data distributions, commonly referred to as concept drift. The presence of concept drift poses a major challenge for many real-world applications because it can severely degrade their predictive performance, hindering their ability to support robust decision-making. Consequently, the timely and efficient detection of drift events is critical for sustaining high accuracy over time. This study examines theoretically the concept drift characteristics and numerous drift detection algorithms across several categories. Furthermore, we evaluate their performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse streaming scenarios and drift characteristics, such as abrupt and gradual changes. This study aims to enhance understanding of the complex notion of concept drift characteristics and behavior of drift detectors, along with their applicability to diverse contexts.

processed.summary:
- The study evaluates **learner-based concept drift detection** algorithms designed to maintain model performance in non-stationary streaming environments.
- It provides a theoretical analysis of **drift characteristics**, specifically focusing on the architectural differences between detectors handling **abrupt versus gradual** distribution shifts.
- The research investigates how different detection categories impact **predictive robustness** and the ability of models to support long-term decision-making.
- It highlights the critical role of timely drift detection in preventing the **accuracy degradation** typically seen in evolving real-world data streams.
key_results: Comprehensive benchmarking of drift detection algorithms across both synthetic and real-world datasets.
Identification of specific performance trade-offs between detectors when facing abrupt versus gradual drift types.
Theoretical mapping of how diverse streaming scenarios influence the sensitivity and latency of drift detectors.
Demonstration that efficient drift detection is the primary factor for sustaining high accuracy in non-stationary environments.
Guidance on the selection of optimal detection strategies based on the specific behavior of the data stream distribution.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers analyze concept drift detection methods to maintain model accuracy and robust decision-making in evolving streaming environments.

title: Accelerating Trust Convergence in IIoT: A ML Approach for Dynamic Network Conditions
summary: In Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) environments, trust management plays a vital role in securing systems, especially when dealing with resource-constrained devices. Traditional trust models often overlook the impact of fluctuating network quality, leading to slower trust convergence and inaccurate assessments. In this paper, we propose a dynamic trust management solution, known as the Trust Convergence Acceleration (TCA) approach, which integrates Machine Learning (ML) to accelerate trust convergence under poor network conditions. Our model predicts the number of time units needed for trust convergence based on key network metrics and dynamically adapts transition probabilities in the trust model to enhance convergence speed. Using a simulation framework that incorporates realistic Wi-Fi channel conditions based on the IEEE 802.11 standard, we demonstrate the effectiveness of the TCA-based approach, achieving up to a 28.6% reduction in trust convergence time under challenging conditions. Furthermore, the proposed solution exhibits resilience in scenarios involving malicious nodes, improving trust evaluation accuracy. This work provides a scalable and adaptive trust framework for IIoT systems in dynamic industrial environments, ensuring robust performance under varying network conditions.

processed.summary:
- Proposes the **Trust Convergence Acceleration (TCA)** framework, which integrates **Machine Learning** to optimize trust management for resource-constrained **IIoT** devices.
- Employs a predictive model to estimate trust convergence timing based on real-time **network metrics**, allowing the system to dynamically adjust **transition probabilities**.
- Utilizes a simulation framework mimicking **IEEE 802.11** Wi-Fi channel conditions to ensure the model remains robust under fluctuating industrial environments.
- Improves overall **system security** by accelerating the detection and isolation of **malicious nodes** through faster and more accurate trust assessments.
key_results: Achieved up to a 28.6% reduction in trust convergence time under poor network conditions.
Improved trust evaluation accuracy in scenarios involving malicious node interference.
Successfully mapped network quality fluctuations to trust model transition probabilities using ML.
Demonstrated scalability for resource-constrained devices in dynamic industrial environments.
Validated performance using realistic Wi-Fi channel simulations based on IEEE standards.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the TCA framework, using ML to accelerate IIoT trust convergence by 28.6% under dynamic conditions.

title: Augmenting Game AI with Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Immersion in video games depends not only on graphics, audio, and game mechanics, but also on the quality of in-game characters. Producing believable characters, or game AI, remains a significant challenge as behavioral complexity is hard to capture with hand-coded systems. Game AI is a source of immersion and engagement; however, the limitations stemming from the challenges of creating game AI often lead to frustration and the breaking of the illusion of realism within the game. The introduction of machine learning models opens the door to creating more believable, authentic, and relatable characters in games. The promise is that they either learn from interacting with the game, or from player data, to develop true human-like behavior. In this paper, we envision more applications of reinforcement learning for game AI in the future. For this to materialize, current research limitations are prohibitive to broad deployment across game genres. Therefore, we propose a framework for training reinforcement learning models with a set of requirements in mind that are suited towards game AI and game development. We present examples of games with reinforcement learning-augmented game AI and describe the practicalities of deploying player-facing machine learning agents in modern games. Furthermore, we identify bottlenecks and hard problems in these areas, which we believe offer promising research directions to accelerate the adoption of machine learning in game AI for the video game industry.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a technical framework for integrating **Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)** into game development to replace or augment traditional, brittle hand-coded systems.
• Focuses on the **architectural requirements** for training agents that can learn from both simulated environments and real-world player data to achieve human-like behavioral complexity.
• Details the **deployment practicalities** of player-facing machine learning agents, emphasizing the need for models that maintain immersion without breaking game logic.
• Identifies critical **research bottlenecks** in data efficiency and training stability that currently prevent broad industry adoption across diverse game genres.
key_results: Identified behavioral limitations of hand-coded systems as the primary cause of immersion-breaking in modern games.
Established a set of technical requirements for RL models to be viable in commercial game production pipelines.
Demonstrated methods for agents to develop authentic behaviors through interaction-based learning.
Mapped out the infrastructure needed to deploy low-latency ML agents in real-time game engines.
Categorized specific industry bottlenecks that represent high-value research directions for AI researchers.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a framework for training reinforcement learning agents to create more authentic and human-like game characters.

title: FlowMaps: Modeling Long-Term Multimodal Object Dynamics with Flow Matching
summary: Joint spatial and temporal understanding of 3D scenes is a crucial requirement for robots deployed in everyday household environments. Such agents must not only comprehend and navigate spatial layouts, but also reason about how these spaces evolve over time. In particular, humans interact with objects daily, causing them to change position throughout the environment and making it difficult for robots to reliably associate current observations with previously seen objects. However, these interactions are not random: human habits and routines induce spatio-temporally consistent patterns in object locations, which robotic agents can potentially learn and then exploit for downstream tasks such as navigation. To this end, we introduce FlowMaps, a latent flow matching model for estimating multimodal distributions over the future locations of dynamic objects in a continuous 3D space. By learning the implicit dependencies among objects and their temporal evolution, FlowMaps predicts likely changes in object locations conditioned on past human interactions, while supporting generalization across previously unseen environments that share similar object routines. To demonstrate the utility of this method, we deploy FlowMaps in a downstream dynamic Object Navigation task in both simulated and real-world environments. Across more than 600 episodes, FlowMaps outperforms state-of-the-art approaches, showing that modeling object dynamics through continuous, multimodal spatio-temporal distributions improves robotic search and navigation in changing household environments. Code and additional material is available at https://fra-tsuna.github.io/flowmaps/.

processed.summary:
- **FlowMaps** introduces a novel **latent flow matching model** designed to estimate multimodal probability distributions of 3D object locations over time.
- The architecture captures **spatio-temporal consistency** by learning implicit dependencies between human routines and object displacement patterns.
- By modeling dynamics in a **continuous latent space**, the system enables robotic agents to predict future object states and generalize to unseen environments.
- The approach moves beyond point-estimates, providing a **multimodal distribution** that accounts for the inherent uncertainty in human-object interactions.
key_results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance in dynamic Object Navigation across 600+ episodes.
Successfully modeled complex, non-Gaussian distributions of object locations in 3D space.
Demonstrated robust zero-shot generalization to new environments with similar routines.
Validated performance in both simulated environments and real-world robotic deployments.
Improved robotic search efficiency by effectively leveraging learned human-induced object patterns.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: University of Bologna researchers introduce FlowMaps, using flow matching to model multimodal object dynamics for improved robotic navigation.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Measuring Logical Compliance of Predictive Models
summary: Machine learning models are predominantly evaluated through predictive performance metrics such as ranking quality, prediction error, or classification accuracy. While these metrics effectively quantify how closely predictions match the ground truth, they do not assess whether model outputs respect predefined logical or domain-specific constraints. In high-stakes applications, including healthcare, finance, and autonomous systems, logical consistency can be as critical as predictive accuracy, yet no standard metric captures this dimension. We introduce the Rule Violation Score (RVS), a complementary evaluation metric that quantifies the extent to which a predictive model respects a given set of logical rules, independently of predictive accuracy. RVS treats hard rules (strict constraints) and soft rules (statistical regularities) differently, can be evaluated on any dataset and on any predictive model expressed over a relational vocabulary, and can be computed using SQL queries that are automatically generated for Horn rules. Beyond evaluating models, RVS can also evaluate the logical consistency of training datasets and help identify poorly defined rules. We evaluate RVS on three benchmarks covering knowledge graph link prediction and relational regression, including rule-based, embedding-based, and neuro-symbolic predictive models. Our results demonstrate that two models achieving comparable predictive accuracy can exhibit substantially different levels of logical compliance, revealing differences in model behavior that standard metrics fail to capture.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the **Rule Violation Score (RVS)**, a novel evaluation metric designed to measure how well predictive models adhere to **logical and domain-specific constraints**.
- Implements an automated **SQL-based framework** for Horn rules, enabling scalable evaluation of both **hard constraints** and **soft statistical regularities** in relational datasets.
- Demonstrates that standard accuracy metrics often fail to capture **logical inconsistencies**, highlighting critical reliability gaps in high-stakes applications like healthcare and finance.
key_results: RVS effectively decouples predictive accuracy from logical compliance measurement.
Models with near-identical accuracy scores exhibit significantly different levels of rule violation.
The framework provides automatic SQL query generation for evaluating Horn rules over relational vocabularies.
RVS can be utilized to audit training datasets for internal logical consistency and rule quality.
Neuro-symbolic and rule-based models generally outperform pure embedding-based models in logical adherence.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose the Rule Violation Score to evaluate logical consistency in predictive models beyond standard accuracy metrics.

title: Optimal multi-spectral squeezing via deterministic 2D-phase optimization
summary: Optimization routines are ubiquitous in quantum information technologies and essential to reach the resource levels required by quantum protocols. Specifically, multi-spectral squeezing for use in such protocols requires that losses be kept minimal at every stage, including coherent detection, which is performed by interfering the signal with a classical local-oscillator beam. This in turn requires control over all optical degrees of freedom of the beam in order to optimize the detection. The most general framework for this optimization relies on agnostic, off-the-shelf machine-learning techniques. Here we take the opposite approach: by focusing on a physical description of the specific optical process, we develop a deterministic sequential algorithm that provably reaches the global maximum of the visibility in a pixel basis and scales linearly with the number of pixels, thereby offering an efficient and theoretically grounded alternative to black-box optimization. In our waveguide-based setup, the optimized mask increases the visibility from 76% to 84%, corresponding to a 20% gain in mode-matching efficiency. Multi-spectral squeezing measurements confirm that this improvement translates directly into quantum readout: for the most squeezed spectral mode, the squeezing increases from $-2.08$ dB to $-2.64$ dB, consistent with the inferred efficiency gain. These results establish deterministic spatial phase shaping as an effective, interpretable route to enhanced multimode squeezing in waveguide platforms.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a **deterministic sequential algorithm** for 2D-phase optimization in quantum waveguides, replacing standard black-box machine learning routines with a physically grounded approach.
* Focuses on maximizing **mode-matching efficiency** by optimizing the interference between signal and local-oscillator beams at every stage of detection.
* Utilizes a framework that scales **linearly with pixel count**, ensuring computational efficiency while provably reaching the global maximum visibility.
* Demonstrates that **spatial phase shaping** directly improves quantum readout performance, reducing detection losses in multi-spectral squeezing protocols.
key_results: Increased optical visibility from 76% to 84% in a waveguide-based setup.
Achieved a 20% gain in mode-matching efficiency through optimized masking.
Improved quantum squeezing from -2.08 dB to -2.64 dB in the most squeezed spectral mode.
Developed an algorithm that reaches global maximum visibility in a pixel basis.
Validated that physical-description-based optimization outperforms agnostic black-box ML techniques for this domain.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a deterministic phase-optimization algorithm to improve quantum multi-spectral squeezing efficiency without black-box machine learning.

title: Evaluating and Enhancing Negation Comprehension in Remote Sensing MLLMs
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated remarkable success in various Remote Sensing (RS) tasks. However, their ability to comprehend negation remains underexplored, limiting deployment in real-world applications where models must explicitly identify what is false or absent, e.g., emergency responders need to locate non-flooded routes for evacuation. To comprehensively study this limitation, we introduce RS-Neg, the first benchmark to evaluate negation understanding across region-level to scene-level tasks. Specifically, we design an automated data generation pipeline for RS imagery, using LLMs to synthesize diverse negation queries, and introduce a dynamic visual focus module for verification. Our evaluation reveals that advanced RS MLLMs struggle with negation, exhibiting hallucinations and substantial performance degradation. To close this gap, we propose NeFo, a novel test-time learning method that explicitly incorporates the logical role of negation into the model optimization. Remarkably, using about 5\% unlabeled test samples, NeFo significantly improves the negation understanding of models and shows strong generalization to unseen tasks. Code and data will be released upon acceptance.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **RS-Neg**, the first benchmark specifically designed to assess **negation comprehension** in Remote Sensing Multimodal Large Language Models (RS MLLMs).
• Implements an **automated data generation pipeline** using LLMs for synthesis and a **dynamic visual focus module** to ensure logical consistency in negation-based queries.
• Proposes **NeFo**, a novel **test-time learning (TTL)** optimization method that explicitly integrates the logical role of negation into model weights during inference.
• Targets the reduction of **visual hallucinations** in RS applications, such as identifying non-flooded routes or missing infrastructure in satellite imagery.
key_results: Advanced RS MLLMs exhibit severe performance degradation and frequent hallucinations when processing negation queries.
NeFo significantly improves negation understanding using only approximately 5% of unlabeled test samples.
The dynamic visual focus module effectively filters and verifies synthetic negation data for RS imagery.
Proposed methods show strong generalization capabilities when applied to previously unseen remote sensing tasks.
The RS-Neg benchmark provides a new standard for evaluating logical reasoning across region-level and scene-level RS tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce NeFo to improve negation comprehension in Remote Sensing MLLMs through targeted test-time learning optimization.

title: Computational Methods and Challenges in Cell-Free DNA Analysis for Multi-Cancer Early Detection
summary: Cell-free DNA (cfDNA) is a promising avenue for non-invasive multicancer early detection (MCED), in that, it can enable multiple cancer detection simultaneously from a single blood draw, with particular sensitivity to cancers that currently lack established screening programs. Here we review the computational methods developed between 2022 and 2025 for cfDNA-based MCED. We focus on how fragmentomics and epigenetic features are extracted and analyzed to detect cancer at early stages. We first briefly outline the biological basis of cfDNA signals, then review classical statistical and machine learning approaches alongside deep learning frameworks including autoencoder-based models. For each method we discuss biological interpretability, validation strategy, and readiness for clinical integration. Furthermore, we categorize the current challenges into technical, computational, and methodological while outlining open problems in the field. This review shows that multimodal ensemble approaches have the strongest promise for clinical integration and the highest readiness. However, for better assessment of future work and side-by-side comparison, standardization of evaluation protocols and reporting results will be crucial.
